# 📘 Instruction Manual

# **Project Ignite Instruction Manual**

**Welcome to Project Ignite\!** We’re excited to have you join us in building high-quality data for the next-generation of AI agents. In this project, you’ll take on the role of a task designer \- creating realistic workplace scenarios, writing expert-level solutions, and defining clear evaluation criteria. Your contributions directly shape how well AI agents can understand, reason, and act within real-world business environments. This manual will guide you through each step of the process so you can produce tasks that are accurate, rigorous, and grounded in authentic company contexts.

**⚠️ Before you start:** Please make sure you have completed all onboarding steps, outlined in detail [here](). 

## **1\. Introduction**

### **About Project Ignite**

Project Ignite is an initiative designed to advance the frontier of AI agent performance. An AI agent is a large language model configured to operate like a capable professional assistant: it can interpret complex prompts, reason through multi-step tasks, interact with relevant files and tools, and produce high-quality, actionable outputs.

Our goal is to evaluate agents’ capabilities by placing them inside realistic company environments and challenging them with authentic, domain-specific work. This project focuses specifically on different types of businesses and the SaaS software / application platforms they rely on. This can range from messaging tools, such as Slack and MS Teams to software development tools such as Github and Bitbucket. By testing a model's ability to execute real-life tasks end-to-end, we can measure how effectively AI agents perform when faced with the kinds of problems businesses encounter every day.

You have been onboarded to simulate real workplace asks that a user of an AI agent could conceivably ask on a day to day basis. We encourage you to leverage your real life and professional experiences when selecting a user persona to complete these tasks, but also are open to you trying out different user personas throughout this project as well. 

## **2\. Important Definitions** {#2.-important-definitions}

Please read the definitions below carefully before you begin; they are essential for completing the task successfully. 

**Why do you need to know these definitions?**   
When you begin writing, you will claim a task from a list of task placeholders that look like this.  
![][image1]

The **task name is the authoritative signal of what the task must contain. It determines the scope, intent, and type of work required in the Prompt and the Golden Response.**

In other words: the Task Name defines what needs to go into the task, and your writing must consistently reflect that definition.

Each task follows the following naming convention: 

**1\_\[WORLD\]\_\[DIFFICULTY\]\_\[PERSONA\]\_\[CUJ\]\_\[TASK NUMBER\]**

In the following sections, each component of the task is defined. When claiming a task, you will need to consider the combination of all components. 

**What are “Worlds”?** 

A *World* is a base set of files and attributes that the model can access. Think of it like a company environment where the model can use the company’s data and tools. It is a self-contained workplace: the model operates as if it is inside that organization, using its data and documents to complete tasks. Worlds provide realistic context and constraints, enabling tasks that reflect how professionals interact with actual company information. 

As a writer, **you will be assigned to a specific world**. Focusing on one world allows you to immerse yourself in its business context and produce realistic, fine-grained tasks.

Each world has **dedicated Slack channels** where you can ask world-specific questions (e.g., about file details) and collaborate with other writers.

**What are “Job Personas”?**   
As a writer, you will adopt a role-specific professional identity in a world to create tasks. Each persona represents an employee within a company (e.g. a financial analyst, operations manager, HR specialist, or product designer) with the corresponding responsibilities, domain knowledge, and decision-making authority. Please see the [Persona Profile Overview]() for more information. 

You need to craft prompts as if you *are* this employee, performing realistic, day-to-day work tasks within that role. By grounding tasks in specific job personas, we ensure:

* **Clear scope of work:** Tasks stay aligned with the actual responsibilities and expertise of that role.  
* **Realistic professional context:** Prompts reflect the decisions, analyses, and outputs a real employee would produce.  
* **Reduced overlap:** Different personas lead naturally to diverse task types, minimizing redundancy across writers.


Job Personas anchor task creation in real-world practice, producing distinct, domain-appropriate assignments that simulate how professionals work inside an organization.

When creating a task, you must ensure that the Prompt and Golden Response remain fully consistent with the responsibilities and perspective of the selected Job Persona.

For example:

* If you are writing for a Financial Analyst, the task should involve financial modeling, reporting, forecasting, or analytical interpretation \- not HR policy writing, IT troubleshooting, or executive-level strategy setting.  
* If the persona is an Operations Manager, the task should reflect operational oversight, efficiency improvements, resource coordination, or process management.

In short: **Every task must authentically reflect what someone in that job would realistically do. Avoid drifting into responsibilities outside the persona’s domain or authority.**

**What are “Customer User Journeys”?**  
You can think of a Customer User Journey (CUJ) as a genre of task in a business environment. The CUJs are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive, meaning that all tasks should be able to fit into one of the CUJ buckets. Here are the four CUJs for this project:

* **Strategy and Planning**: defining long-term direction, priorities, and what the organization should do.  
* **Operations and Execution**: running day-to-day processes to deliver work efficiently and reliably.  
* **Analysis, Research, and Benchmarking**: generating insights and evidence to inform decisions and evaluate performance.  
* **People, Communications, and Personal Productivity**: managing relationships, alignment, and individual effectiveness in getting work done.

When creating a task, you must adhere strictly to the CUJ (Critical User Journey) definition associated with that task. This means the Prompt and Golden Response must align with the specific CUJ category.

For example:

* If a task is labeled “Strategy & Planning,” then the prompt you write must explicitly involve defining long-term direction, priorities, and what the organization should do.  
* Do not create a prompt that focuses on execution, analysis, or operational details if the CUJ is about strategic planning.

In short: **The content of the task must accurately reflect the CUJ it is associated with—no mixing or drifting into other CUJs.**

### **Important Note on Alignment with CUJs and Job Personas**

When creating a task, you must adhere strictly to the CUJ (Critical User Journey) definition and the Job Persona associated with that task.

This means the Prompt and Golden Response must accurately reflect the intersection of both:

* the **CUJ category** (what type of action or cognitive work is being performed), and  
* the **Job Persona** (who is performing it and what responsibilities, authority, and expertise they realistically have).

For example:

* If a task is labeled “Strategy & Planning” and the persona is an Operations Manager, the prompt must involve long-term operational direction, prioritization, and defining what the operations function should do—not financial analysis, HR policy drafting, or low-level execution tasks.  
* If the CUJ is “Execution & Implementation” but the persona is a Director-level role, the prompt should reflect high-level oversight of implementation rather than hands-on tasks that an individual contributor would perform.

**What is the “Difficulty level”?**  
There are 3 possible difficulty levels. 

**Common**: Simple lookups from a single data source, with 1-2 simple operations to be conducted on the data.

**Advanced**: Lookups from multiple data sources **OR** external tools or data to be integrated into relevant calculations **OR** stronger levels of synthesis requested

**Hard**: At least two of the three following items: Lookups from multiple data sources, external tools or data to be integrated into relevant calculations, stronger levels of synthesis requested

## 

## **3\. Step-by-Step Workflow** {#3.-step-by-step-workflow}

All tasks must be completed in RL Studio. Please see the [RL Studio Guide]() for further details on navigating the platform. 

1️⃣ **Analyze the files in your world**   
Pick a world in RL Studio. Examine the files to understand what kind of business this is, and what data is available. Note key entities (teams, customers, products) and any recurring metrics, processes, or problems that could be turned into realistic tasks. When you begin, expect to spend **roughly 1 hour** reviewing the world files.

As you read, ask yourself:

* What is the business model? What does this company sell or provide?  
* Who are their customers or stakeholders?  
* What workflows do they rely on?  
* What recurring challenges or goals appear in the documents (e.g. growth, churn, operations issues, hiring, compliance)?


It might be helpful to note down observations that you can refer back to when designing your tasks.

To open any world files, please see the [How To Read CSV Files Guide](#4.-how-to-read-csv-files) below. It is essential you follow this guide precisely\! How you open the file has an impact on how accurate your Golden Response is, which will be further explained below. 

2️⃣ **Claim a task**   
Follow the [RL Studio Guide](#4.-how-to-read-csv-files) for claiming a task and the above defined definitions for understanding what each task is about. 

Task names have already been defined for you. Please **do not change** the task name that is already included. To claim a task, please **only add your name in brackets** behind the existing task name, **without removing** any other information. 

For example, a task is called: **1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]**

Add your name in the following way to claim it: **1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]\_\[First Name\_Last Name\]**

Once you add your name, the task is officially assigned to you. Do not work on any task that already has a name in the title, and do not claim more than one task at a time. Please see the section below for guidance on what RL Studio Task Fields you need to fill out (and which ones you can ignore) 

3️⃣ **Research your job persona and CUJ**  
Once you have picked a task, you should make sure to: 

* Read each world’s [persona descriptions]()   
* Infer persona responsibilities based on the world files   
* Consider what information, tools, and authority this persona could realistically have

For your persona, you should be able to answer: “What are my day-to-day tasks? What decisions or outputs am I accountable for?”

Use that understanding to guide the tasks you create.

4️⃣ **Create a task prompt**  
Now, write a task prompt from the perspective of your persona, and consider the CUJ. Additionally, each task should be categorized according to the following difficulties: **Common, Advanced, and Hard**. The prompt should meet all guidelines outlined in the [Writing Task Prompts Guide](). Based on our experience, aim to average **\~45 min** per task. Please also see the [RL Studio Guide]() for guidance on how to name your tasks and what fields you will have to fill out.   
Overall, the prompt should: 

* Be framed as a realistic request for your persona  
* Refer directly to the company’s context, data, or files when appropriate  
* Be specific about the desired output format  
* Require multi-step, realistic work (not trivial lookups). The agent should have to read, synthesize, reason, or make decisions using the world’s files.

If this task could be assigned to a real human employee in that role and take them some meaningful effort, it’s likely a strong task prompt.

5️⃣ **Upload the Original Task Input File you used**  
From the World Files, download the Task Input Files you used. Re-upload it in the Task Input File section below the Task Prompt in RL Studio in **its original, unchanged form**. This indicates what sources you used. **Do not upload the reformatted csv file.** 

6️⃣ **List all file and record names in the “Notes” Section**   
In the Notes field below the Task Input File field, please list all the files you used, using the format: **\[file name: record \#\] formatting).** There is nothing else needed in the Notes section besides this list. Please see the [Golden Response Guide]() for the definitions of record and the  [How To Read CSV Files Guide](#4.-how-to-read-csv-files) to ensure you are using the correct record number.   
**![][image2]**

7️⃣ **Write the Golden Response**  
For each task prompt, write a golden response: this is the solution guide to the ideal answer a highly capable agent should produce. The Golden Response should meet all guidelines outlined in the Golden Response Guide. Use and refer to the files in the World’s data room. Before you write the Golden Response, please see the How To Read CSV Files Guide below. It is essential you follow this guide precisely\! How you open the file has an impact on how accurate the data referencing in your Golden Response is, which will be further explained in the Golden Response Guide. 

Overall, the golden response should: 

* Fully answer the task as written, using the world’s information correctly and explicitly when relevant.  
* Demonstrate strong reasoning, structure, clarity, and professional tone consistent with the persona and company context.  
* Include all required components, including the data notation 

**8️⃣ Check your answers**  
After completing all fields, take a brief moment to check your answers. Make sure: 

* All fields are complete and match their intended purpose  
* The task and golden response are aligned and fit the world/persona context  
* There are no grammatical or spelling mistakes 

A reviewer will then pick up your task and verify whether your task meets all guidelines. 

**9️⃣ Integrate reviewer feedback**   
Once your reviewer has left comments or change requests:

* Carefully read all feedback and clarify (for yourself) what needs to change  
* Update the prompt and golden response directly in RL Studio to address each point.  
* Double-check that your revisions didn’t introduce new inconsistencies or errors.  
* If reviewers suggest a problematic pattern (e.g., prompts too vague), apply that feedback to your **future tasks**, not just the current one.

**Expect to go through at least 1-2 review cycles,** especially if you have not worked on a Mercor project before. 

## 

## **4\. How To Read CSV Files**  {#4.-how-to-read-csv-files}

To ensure every writer works with **identical cell names, column headers, and row structures**, follow the steps below **exactly** when downloading and preparing CSV files.

1. **Download the file only as CSV from the World in RL Studio**   
     
2. **Import it in a way that prevents auto-formatting:**

When opening the CSV in Excel, Google Sheets, or another spreadsheet tool:  
Disable automatic formatting (e.g., date conversion, number formatting, delimiter guessing)  
If your tool has an “Import” or “Upload” workflow, select options like:

* Plain text import  
* Do not detect data types  
* Keep original formatting

**The goal is:** what’s in the CSV must appear exactly the same in your sheet. 

3. **Preserve the File Structure Exactly**  
   Once the CSV is open:  
* **Do NOT add any rows above the data**  
* **Do NOT add any columns to the left of the table**  
* **Do NOT reorder or rename any columns**  
* **Do NOT delete or insert rows**  
  The first row should remain the **original column headers**, and all rows must stay exactly where they are to ensure accurate cell referencing later (see Golden Response Guide)  
  To widen a column in Excel, move your mouse to the right edge of the column header until a double-sided arrow appears, then click and drag to adjust the width. You can also double-click the edge to auto-fit the column to its contents. Alternatively, use **Home \> Format \> Column Width** to enter an exact width.

# 🌍 World & Persona Profiles

# **World & Persona Profiles**

CUJs: *all tasks should be able to fit into one of these buckets*

* **Strategy and Planning**: defining long-term direction, priorities, and what the organization should do.  
* **Operations and Execution**: running day-to-day processes to deliver work efficiently and reliably.  
* **Analysis, Research, and Benchmarking**: generating insights and evidence to inform decisions and evaluate performance.  
* **People, Communications, and Personal Productivity**: managing relationships, alignment, and individual effectiveness in getting work done.

\*\*\*  
Persona Profiles are *coming soon*. In the meantime, following are some examples of what these might look like:

# 🖥️ RL Studio Guide

# **RL Studio Guide** 

## **🧭 How to Use RL Studio for Completing Tasks**

### **1\. Get Access**

To join the platform, go to [studio.mercor.com](http://studio.mercor.com) and “Sign Up” with your **personal** **email** associated with your Mercor account. 

![][image3]

If you aren’t able to sign in or there are no campaigns assigned to you when you login, reach out to the project leads to get access.

### **2\. Go to the “Writing Tasks” Tab**

Once you’re on the platform, click on the **“Writing Tasks”** tab in the main menu.

![][image4]

### **3\. Select a “World”**

In the top-right corner, open the drop-down menu to choose a **World**.

A *World* is a base set of files and attributes the model can access — think of it like a company environment where the model has that company’s data and tools.

![][image5]

### **4\. Select a Task**

Templates for all required task types have already been created. Please refer to the [Definitions Guide](#2.-important-definitions), then choose a task definition you feel confident completing.

![][image6]

### **5\. Claim a Task**

Task names have already been defined for you. Please **do not change** the task name that is already included. To claim a task, please **only add your name in brackets** behind the existing task name, **without removing** any other information. 

For example, a task is called: **1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]**

Add your name in the following way to claim it: **1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]\_\[First Name\_Last Name\]**

Once you add your name, the task is officially assigned to you. Do not work on any task that already has a name in the title, and do not claim more than one task at a time. Please see the section below for guidance on what RL Studio Task Fields you need to fill out (and which ones you can ignore) 

## **RL Studio Task Fields**

### **EDIT: Task Name**

Task names have already been defined for you. Please **do not change** the task name that is already included. To claim a task, please **only add your name in brackets** behind the existing task name, **without removing** any other information. 

E.g. A task is called: **1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]**

Add your name in the following way:   
**1\_\[wing\]\_\[common\]\_\[persona\]\_\[Strategy\&Planning\]\_\[task\#1\]\_\[First Name\_Last Name\]**

**EDIT: Task Prompt**  
Enter your task prompt in this box. See the [Writing Task Prompts Guide]() for specific instructions. 

**UPLOAD: Task Input Files**   
From the World Files, download the Task Input Files you used. Re-upload it in the Task Input File section below the Task Prompt in RL Studio in **its original, unchanged form**. This indicates what sources you used. **Do not upload the reformatted csv file.** 

**EDIT: Notes**   
Write all record names you used for the Prompt and Golden response in the “Notes” Section (using \[file name: record \#\] formatting). See the [Golden Response Guide]() for further information on what a record is.  

**EDIT: Golden Response**  
Here, you must input your own response to the task prompt you’ve created. If your task requires a textual output, please enter it into the “Response Text” box. If it requires file handling, then upload the file by selecting “add file” in “Response Files,” and clicking “Save changes” in the Golden Response window. See [Golden Response]() for specific instructions. 

**DO NOT EDIT: Run Trajectory, Rubric, Grade** 

✅ **That’s it\!**

You’re now ready to start writing tasks on the platform. Make sure to click the Save Button for each field you fill out (always at the top right of each section). 

![][image7]

# ✍️ Creating Tasks

# **Writing Task Prompts** 

When creating a task, you must adhere strictly to the Job Persona, CUJ (Critical User Journey) definition and difficulty level, associated with the task you claimed (see [Step-by-Step Guide](#3.-step-by-step-workflow)) 

This means the Prompt and Golden Response must accurately reflect the intersection of:

* the **CUJ category** (what type of action or cognitive work is being performed), and  
* the **Job Persona** (who is performing it and what responsibilities, authority, and expertise they realistically have).  
* The **difficulty** (common, advanced, hard)

## **1\. Content Guidelines** 

Your prompt is the foundation of the entire task. A high-quality prompt should feel like a realistic request a professional in your selected persona would make while working inside the simulated company environment. Sticking closely to your persona is crucial – if you drift outside their role, it is more likely tasks become generic and overlap across writers, which reduces the diversity and usefulness of the dataset.

Each prompt must rely on the world’s files, require genuine analysis or decision-making appropriate for your selected task scope, and be specific enough that an experienced human could produce a complete, correct answer. Overall, the prompt should:

* Be framed as a natural request the persona would make  
* Refer directly to the company’s context, data, or files when appropriate  
* Be specific about the desired output format

The following guidelines outline the core qualities every task prompt must meet.

| Representative and Expertise-Driven  | Prompts must reflect authentic tasks performed in the profession and require domain-specific knowledge, judgment, and technical skill. They should lead to meaningful, work-relevant outcomes that cannot be solved through general reasoning alone. |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Includes a Clear Purpose (“So What?”)** | Each prompt should state or imply why the task matters—what decision it supports, what problem it solves, or how the output will be used. |
| **Well-Defined and Professionally Answerable** | The prompt must be precise, unambiguous, and structured so that a seasoned expert can provide a complete and correct response when given the required context or files. |
| **Requires World Input Files (min. 1\)** | The task must depend on at least one file provided in the environment. The agent should need to reference, analyze, or extract information from the file(s) to complete the task fully. |
| **Difficult**  | This project will require a variety of difficulty-levels in prompts, ranging from simple tasks to prompts that challenge the agent in a meaningful way. **Please see below** for information on how to determine difficulty. |
| **Requires Multi-Step Reasoning** | Prompts should require multiple analytical or computational steps (typically three or more). They must avoid trivial tasks that can be solved in one or two steps. |
| **Supports Objective Evaluation and Minimizes Subjectivity** | The task should lead to an answer that experts would broadly agree on, or one that can be evaluated using clear, objective criteria. Tasks should not rely on personal taste, stylistic preference, or highly subjective judgment. |
| **Open-ended vs. closed-ended** | Prompts may be open-ended (allowing multiple valid expert approaches) or close-ended (requiring a specific solution), but in both cases the evaluation criteria must remain objective and grounded in domain standards. An open-ended task: Adheres to professional industry standards Can likely be done by one person (non-collaborative) Can be done in one sitting A closed-ended task: Has one correct, verifiable answer |

**Can we assume the agent has web access when designing prompts and golden responses?**   
Yes. Anything you could normally ask ChatGPT you can assume this model should be able to do. e.g. if you need to convert from euros to dollars, you can google that. Just define it in the steps.

## **2\. Determining Difficulty**

Below are examples of task variations ranging from Common to Hard. These are not hard and fast rules (“hard” tasks can be more complex, for example), but meant to give you a rough idea of what to aim for.

\*\*NOTE: Difficulty is not about how hard it is for the expert, more about how difficult it is for the model

**Common**: Simple lookups from a single data source, with 1-2 simple operations to be conducted on the data.

I would like to know how much revenue we got in the month of October after the 8th, according to our invoices."

**Advanced**: Lookups from multiple data sources **OR** external tools or data to be integrated into relevant calculations **OR** stronger levels of synthesis requested

"I would like to know how much revenue we got in the month of October after the 8th, according to our invoices. Provide the results in USD assuming usd/nzd \= 1.73"

**Hard**: At least two of the three following items: Lookups from multiple data sources, external tools or data to be integrated into relevant calculations, stronger levels of synthesis requested

 "I would like to know how much net revenue we got in the month of October from 10/8-31, according to our invoices. Subtract 15% NZ Goods and Services Tax from NZ invoices and provide the results in USD assuming usd/nzd \= 1.73. Compare this result to 10/8-31 of 2024 and provide a quick summary."

## **3\. Style Guidelines**

It is important that we maintain a clear and consistent writing style across all tasks. Adhere to this style guide in all your tasks.

Tone: 

* **Objective and explanatory:** Use a neutral, fact-based tone focused on conveying information.  
* **No filler content:** Avoid unnecessary commentary or conversational padding that does not contribute to meeting the above content guidelines.  
* **Persona-specific phrasing:** Write in a manner consistent with how someone in that role would typically communicate, using the terminology and style appropriate to that profession and industry.   
* **Third-person style:** Refer to entities directly (e.g., “the formula demonstrates…” rather than “we can see that the formula…”).


**Language:** 

* Precise technical vocabulary: Use correct Domain specific terminology   
  * Finance: e.g., futures contract, debt instrument, interest rate  
  * Legal: e.g., breach of fiduciary duty, due process, statute of limitations  
  * Management Consulting: e.g., value proposition, synergy realization, market segmentation, change management  
* No casual or metaphorical language: Do not use analogies unless absolutely necessary for the prompt.

# 🏅 Golden Response

# **Golden Response Guide** 

## **1\. What is a “Golden Response”?** 

For Project Ignite, a Golden Response is **the solution guide to the ideal answer an expert would expect an AI model to produce for a given prompt and set of files, written by a human.**

A Golden Response should 

* Be complete  
* Be well-structured  
* Be clear & concise  
* Show thinking and steps taken, including which file and record was used (see Structure & Formatting Guidelines below)   
* Be representative of what qualified professionals in the field would independently deliver


Write the highest-quality response you would expect if you had submitted this prompt yourself. Golden Responses are held to a high standard because they are directly compared to strong LLM outputs. Therefore, they should consistently **meet or exceed the quality of state-of-the-art models.**

As you write, imagine a committee of expert peers reviewing your work. Ask yourself: Would most professionals in my field agree this is accurate, thorough, and well-executed?

⚠️ Please ensure that **all responses are created independently** and reflect your own professional expertise. While you may conduct research to inform your work, the use of large language models (LLMs) or any other **AI tools to generate or assist with responses is strictly prohibited**. Submitting AI-generated content may result in removal from the project.

**2\. Why write a Golden Response?**

Golden Responses serve two key purposes:

1. **Clarifying the ideal answer.** Writing a Golden Response helps articulate the highest standard of what the model should ultimately learn to produce, while ensuring you have a clear understanding of what an excellent, expert-level response looks like.  
2. **Assessing the task itself.** Crafting a response helps you determine whether the task is too simple, too broad, or otherwise too challenging to yield a solid answer.

**3\. Golden Response Structure & Formatting Guidelines**

**Step 0: Important Notation Definitions for this Section**  
Please note the difference between file name, record number and relevant data, which are relevant for several steps below.   
 

* **File name:** The name of the document If the file is inside a folder in the World Files, **the file name should include the folder path**. If the file appears directly in the World Files list, no folder path is needed.  
* **Record \#:** The row, column, cell, or unique identifier showing where the information is located in the file   
* **Relevant data:** The specific piece of information identified. *The Relevant Data should only appear in the “Golden Response,” and does not need to be included in the “Notes.”*  
  	\[FileName:Record\#(RelevantData)\]


**For example:**

* **Spreadsheet files (csv, xlsx):**   
* **File name:** NovemberRevenueTracker.csv  
* **Record \#:** the place in the document where you found the data (i.e. the cell identifier)   
  * *Note: this is slightly different for each file. Please see the pinned message in your world Slack channel for guidance on Record \# identifiers and general guidance on file reference notation.*   
* **Relevant data:** $300,000 (i.e., the exact value in the cell) 

  “Total Revenue was identified by extracting \[NovemberRevenueTracker.csv:10008762425($300,000)\]”. 


*Note: In instances where many Records are referenced (e.g. an entire column is read), you can include all record numbers into one file notation. For example:* 

\[unsubscribed\_email\_audience\_export\_d8b2f5d21e-anonymized.csv:  
53b008a0f0(RelevantData1)  
ec70a48a44(RelevantData2)  
09f2ec2b90(RelevantData3)  
32fd38173d(RelevantData4)\]

*\*If you copy and paste column data straight from the file it will appear like this, so no additional formatting is needed.*

* **📖 Text files (PDF, DOCX, etc):**

They should include the Filepath/filename, page number, and the first three words of the relevant section.  \[FileName:Page\#(“First Three Words”)\]

Example:  
\[Snail Drive/Drive/rr-Events/Compatibility List.docx:Page01("First three words")\]  
\[Snail Drive/Drive/rr-ProductCompatibility List.docx:Page01("First three words")\]

* **🌐 Web pages**

Format: \[web link: \*paste entire URL\*\]

Example:   
\[web link: https://u.ae/en/about-the-uae/digital-uae/data/data-protection-laws\]

* **🖼️ Image Files (.png, jpeg, etc):**

Add only the file name (include the filepath as well if there’s one). Example:

\[Figma/links selection design.png\]

**Step 1: Upload all files used to “Task Input File”**  
From the World Files, download the Task Input File(s) you used. Re-upload it in the Task Input File section below the Task Prompt in RL Studio in **its original, unchanged form**. This indicates what sources you used. **Do not upload the reformatted csv file.**   
*This will be the only file upload section of the Golden Response; all references to files beyond this point should be written (as outlined below).* 

![][image8]  
**📁 Filepaths for Files Inside Folders (New Rule)**

When referencing a file located inside a folder, include the folder path in the file name.

**Examples (TSTS):**

* File in the main World Files:  
   `[Stripe_prices.csv:price_1RYlGIDmmWh32CUjgkbLnas2(Large)]`  
* File inside a folder:  
   `[OneShotFinal/OS_Contractors24_3_1.csv:2hohF23d7OQEn6N3ZL7b(Jamie)]`

**Step 2: List all file and record names in the “Notes” Section**   
In the Notes field below the Task Input File field,  **\[file name: record \#\].** There is nothing else needed in the Notes section besides this list.   
**![][image9]**  
Please note: You do not need to include the relevant data section here (only file name and record \#). The relevant data is only included in the Golden Response (see Step 3.2). 

**Step 3: Write the golden response**  
The Golden Response is the final, polished answer that demonstrates *ideal reasoning* and *clear documentation of each step taken*. It must include the following elements:

**Step 3.1: Begin with a concise, high-quality answer to the user’s prompt.**  
This should be the answer to the question or request that the prompt asks. Think of it as the “executive summary.”

**Step 3.2: Full Step-by-Step Reasoning with Required Notation Format**  
After the topline answer, provide a complete walkthrough of the steps taken to reach that conclusion.

**Each step should:**

* Clearly describe the action you performed  
* Reference the exact data used  
* Use the required notation format (see below)

**Step Structure Example**

* Step 1: “First, I identified the file \[file name\].”  
* Step 2: “Then I found the data point \[file name: record \# (relevant data)\].”  
* Step 3: “Next, I analyzed these values and performed xyz calculation.”  
  Make sure every step is explicit and traceable back to the source data.

**Required Notation Format**  
Whenever you reference information from a file, use **\[file name: record \# (relevant data)\]**  
Every time a data point influences your reasoning, include this notation.

**Data Volume Guidance**  
To keep the Golden Response clear and manageable, ensure that prompts do not require referencing an excessively large number of records. Because **every Record must be listed in both the Notes and Golden Response sections**, design prompts that involve about 20 data points or fewer.

If a question would normally require analyzing a large dataset, use a representative sample instead. For example: 

* If determining what percentage of a team has been at a company for more than five years, select 20 representative records, calculate the percentage from that subset, and reference only those 20 records.

**This method ensures:**

* Transparency  
* Auditability  
* The ability for another reviewer to trace each thought back to the data source

A Golden Response should read like a **solution guide** **to the ideal answer** that others can replicate.

**⚠️Very important**: **Your response must always call out the specific data points** you used in each step to make the solution crystal clear. *Tasks that do not call out specific data points (when data has been referenced) will be immediately returned for rework by reviewers.* 

**4\. Golden Response Checklist**

Before submitting the Golden Response, ask yourself: 

**Completeness:** 

* Is the Golden Response complete? (Does it answer all parts of the prompt?)  
* Does it accurately reflect the prompt’s intent?  
* Does it correctly address the world context and all relevant elements?  
* Does it accurately list the steps used to solve the task?  
* Does it correctly call out all the data that was used to solve the task?

**Quality of Writing**

* Is the writing clear, coherent, and easy to follow?  
* Is the response free of grammar, spelling, and punctuation errors?  
* Is the tone appropriate for the task and audience?  
* Is the level of detail suitable for the context (neither too vague nor overly complex)?

**Structure & Formatting**

* Is the response formatted according to the conventions of the task context?  
  (e.g., emails include subject lines, output files are professionally formatted )  
* Is the structure logical and consistent with professional standards in this genre or format?  
* Do all data references stick to the above Structure & Formatting Guidelines?

**Professionalism & Expertise**

* Does the response demonstrate professional-level knowledge and competence?  
* Would a majority of experts in this field agree that this output meets professional standards?

**Execution & Final Presentation**

* Does the response feel polished, intentional, and ready for real-world use?  
* Does it deliver the final output as the user would expect to receive it (not a draft or outline unless requested)?  
* Is the response internally consistent (tone, viewpoint, formatting, factual content)?  
  


# 📝 Notes Section

# **Notes Section Guide**

In the Notes field below the Task Input File field, please list all the files you used, using the format: **\[file name: record \#\].** There is nothing else needed in the Notes section besides this list. NOTE: We have different file formats. Each one requires a respective notation. Please check the pins at your respective world Slack channel for more information if needed**. If the file is inside a folder in the World Files, include the full folder path before the file name when listing it in the Notes.**

1. **🧮 Spreadsheets (csv, xlsx, etc): Row Identifiers**

| 1.1. TSTS:   ![][image10] | 1.2. FQFQ:   ![][image11] |
| :---- | :---- |

**1.3 Legwork (includes filepath)**

**![][image12]**

2. **🌐 Web links : \[web link: \*paste entire URL\*\]**

![][image13]

3. **🖼️ Image Files: Only the path/filename.**   
   

\[Figma/links selection design.png\] 

4. **📖 Text files (PDF, DOCX, etc): Add the relevant pages.**

\[Snail Drive/Drive/rr-Events/External Event Package (after signing).docx:  
Page01  
Page17\]

# 🏆 Examples

# **Golden Examples** 

| Task Name | 32\_\[FQFQ\]\_\[advanced\]\_\[OperationsManager\]\_\[Analysis,Research\&Benchmarking\]\_\[task\#8\]\_\[Juhana Lano\] |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Task Prompt** | I'm investigating whether our development priority assignment is influenced by the estimated development effort, and I need some data to check my hypothesis. Can you calculate the average effort estimate for each priority category (Low/Medium/High/Urgent)? Use a sample of five development items for each priority category. Consider only already done items, as I assume those have the most up-to-date estimates, and take the sample from the most recent development items. Most recent in this case means the highest running ID number, 1Q-4634 being more recent than 1Q-4633, and so on. |
| **Input Files** | Linear Data Uploadable2.csv |
| **Notes** | \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv: 1Q-4573 1Q-4578 1Q-4579 1Q-4582 1Q-4597 1Q-4627 1Q-4628 1Q-4631 1Q-4633 1Q-4634 1Q-4448 1Q-4449 1Q-4549 1Q-4629 1Q-4630 1Q-4382 1Q-4393 1Q-4402 1Q-4405 1Q-4438\] |
| **Golden Response** | The average estimates for each priority category, based on a sample of five most recent development items from the given priority category in "Done" status, are as follows: "Urgent" priority \- average estimate 1.4 "High" priority \- average estimate 2.8 "Medium" priority \- average estimate 2.4 "Low" priority \- average estimate 0.6. 1\. First, I determined that the backlog data containing the estimates can be found in the file "Linear Data Uploadable2.csv". 2\. Then, I validated that the data is sorted based on the "ID" column running number, from smallest to largest. 3\. On my next step, I queried records with priority "Urgent" and status "Done", and selected the five most recent items based on the "ID" column, which resulted in the following estimates: \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4597(1.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4582(2.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4579(2.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4578(1.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4573(1.0)\]. 4\. After that, I calculated the average from the five estimates for "Urgent" priority, which yielded 1.4. 5\. Then, I queried records with priority "High" and status "Done", and selected the five most recent items based on the "ID" column, which resulted in the following estimates: \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4634(3.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4633(3.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4631(3.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4628(3.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4627(2.0)\]. 6\. Using these results, I calculated the average from the five estimates for "High" priority, which yielded 2.8. 7\. Following, I queried records with priority "Medium" and status "Done", and selected the five most recent items based on the "ID" column, which resulted in the following estimates: \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4630(3.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4629(2.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4549(2.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4449(2.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4448(3.0)\]. 8\. Using these results, I calculated the average from the five estimates for "Medium" priority, which yielded 2.4. 9\. After that, I queried records with priority "Low" and status "Done", and selected the five most recent items based on the "ID" column, which resulted in the following estimates: \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4438(1.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4405(1.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4402(0.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4393(0.0)\], \[Linear Data Uploadable2.csv:1Q-4382(1.0)\]. 10\. Using these results, I calculated the average from the five estimates for "Low" priority, which yielded 0.6. 11\. At last, I presented the calculation results for each category on separate lines for clarity. |

| World  | FQFQ |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Task Name** | 33\_\[FQFQ\]\_\[advanced\]\_\[CEO/ExecutiveLeader\]\_\[Analysis,Research\&Benchmarking\]\_\[task\#9\]\_\[Zi Jing\_Kang\] |
| **Task Prompt** | As a new executive leader at FQFQ, a company selling an AI buddy for sales teams, your current OKR is to turn the company around after a financial slump post-COVID-19. Your new initiative is to overhaul your product pricing as you believe it is currently underpriced. Your business analysts and marketing researchers analyzed competitors and discovered that for a product like yours, which boosts productivity by \~35%, companies in Switzerland (your direct market) are willing to spend maximally 20% of a sales executive's wage for a monthly subscription to boost their sales performance.  Calculate if your current product subscription pricing is within 10% of what companies are willing to pay at the maximum. Take the average of the recurring payments from 15 March 2022 or later from \`Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv\` as your product subscription price to benchmark against. Do a web search for an official report that reports on the average monthly wage of a sales executive in Switzerland. You can assume the wage of a sales executive in Switzerland to be equal to the average wage in the country.  |
| **Input Files** | Invoices.csv                       This world contains two files named Invoices.csv. Make sure to upload the matching file from the correct folder, without modifying it |
| **Notes** | \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv: AzqNR7Sgnv5fJ8IVL AzZdIISr1Kygtn39 AzqNK2SbfVHtYIBq5 AzqNGQSdbk5PUeDu AzZdExSbtVA7fT6x 169lUASbfXmqBIoQD 169lUASbfUtH3ImOv\] |
| **Golden Response** | Following the given assumptions in your prompt, your current product subscription pricing is not within 10% of what companies are willing to pay at the maximum. Your current average product subscription price is 356 CHF, when accounting for the 10% threshold, gives a range of 320.4 \- 391.6 CHF. 20% of the average monthly wage of a sales executive in Switzerland, according to the Swiss Federal Statistical Office, is 1404.8 CHF, which is much higher than the upper bound of 391.6 CHF in the range mentioned earlier. Step 1\. First, I identified the file \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv\]. Step 2\. Next, I identified and counted the number of invoices that are recurring and occurred from 15 March 2022 or later. looking at the "Invoice Date" and "Recurring" columns. They are:  \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNR7Sgnv5fJ8IVL(3/17/22 18:09, TRUE)\]  \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzZdIISr1Kygtn39(3/16/22 10:10, TRUE)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNK2SbfVHtYIBq5(3/16/22 9:00, TRUE)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNGQSdbk5PUeDu(3/16/22 9:00, TRUE)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzZdExSbtVA7fT6x(3/15/22 9:00, TRUE)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:169lUASbfXmqBIoQD(3/15/22 9:00, TRUE)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:169lUASbfUtH3ImOv(3/15/22 9:00, TRUE)\]. There are 7 invoices. Step 3\. Next, I extracted the payment amount of each of these subscriptions, looking at the "Amount" column, and added them together:   \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNR7Sgnv5fJ8IVL(575)\]  \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzZdIISr1Kygtn39(0)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNK2SbfVHtYIBq5(425)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzqNGQSdbk5PUeDu(468)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:AzZdExSbtVA7fT6x(175)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:169lUASbfXmqBIoQD(402)\], \[Readyinvoices\_product\_29\_Nov\_2022\_17\_46\_07/Invoices.csv:169lUASbfUtH3ImOv(447)\], which makes 2492\. Step 4\. I calculated the average subscription price using the figures derived in steps 2 and 3\. (2492 / 7\) \= 356, which we will assume to be the price we use to benchmark. Step 5\. Next, I did a web search for an official Swiss report for the average monthly wage. \[web link:https://www.bfs.admin.ch/bfs/en/home/statistics/work-income/wages-income-employment-labour-costs.html\] The report stated 7024 Francs, which we will assume is the average monthly wage of a sales executive in Switzerland. Step 6\. Next, I calculated the maximum a company would pay for our product, which is 20% of the figure from step 5\. 20% \* 7024 \= 1404.8. Step 7\. Next, I calculate the acceptable range of our product pricing using the 10% threshold, using (90% \* 356\) and (110% \* 356), to get a range of 320.4 \- 391.6. Step 8\. Lastly, I compare to see if the number derived in step 6 is within the range calculated in step 7\. It is not.  |

# ✈️ Onboarding

# **Onboarding Instructions**

### **Welcome to Project Ignite\!** 

Please follow all instructions below before you start working on tasks. 

**Mandatory Requirements:**

* You are required to contribute **at least 10 hours per week** to Project Ignite.

### **Step 0: Set Up Insightful**

Once you accept the contract, you will receive an email with instructions to set up **Insightful**, our time-tracking tool.

* Please review the [**Insightful guidelines**](https://talent.docs.mercor.com/how-to/use-insightful) for detailed setup and usage instructions.  
* **Download** the **Workpuls** application and start the timer whenever you’re actively working on this project.  
* Log **all active project time** — whether it’s a few seconds or several hours. This includes all meetings, training, and other project-related tasks.

**Notes:**

* **The onboarding call is also eligible for billing time. There will be no compensation for the time spent on the onboarding call if you have not logged the time through Insightful.**   
* Time **not logged through Insightfu**l will **not** be compensated.  
* Idle or non-project work is subject to review and may be deducted.  
* Ensure that the window you’re sharing in Insightful clearly shows your active work.  
* The timer name in Insightful will appear as **“Project Ignite”** — please use this timer for all related contributions.  
* For additional support on setting up Insightful or if you are having issues (e.g., the project not appearing or disappearing from your Insightful, please email [glowstonesupport@mercor.com](mailto:glowstonesupport@mercor.com). 

### **Step 1: Join Slack via Okta**

Slack will be the main platform for project communications. Please Review the [Okta guidelines](https://talent.docs.mercor.com/how-to/okta-access) for detailed setup instructions for accessing the Project Slack.

Once your background verification is complete and you’ve accepted your offer, please complete your **Okta setup** from the **Contract page** on the Mercor website.

After setup, you’ll see the **Slack app** on your Okta dashboard — use it to join the **Project Ignite** Slack workspace.

* The Slack **Announcement channel** is where important Project Ignite updates are posted. Everyone is expected to check this channel regularly. (screenshot)  
* Your **world channel** will be your homebase throughout the project. Get to know your colleagues here\!  
* You can run world specific questions by your world channel, and brainstorm task ideas. It is important to communicate in order to have a healthy variety of tasks in each world and avoid overlap. 

### **Step 2: Join the Project Calendar**

Join our project Ignite Google calendar [here](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/u/2?cid=Y184YWM1ZWIxMzVmNzk2YTQ2NmVmODI1NTQzZjgzOWFjNjI5OGUzNWFmZDhhYzYxZTgzZmU4MzQwMzM2NWI4ZjZmQGdyb3VwLmNhbGVuZGFyLmdvb2dsZS5jb20). All project related meetings and office hours will be scheduled on this calendar. Please keep an eye out for the **mandatory onboarding session.**   
Reach out to the Project Leads if you are not able to join.

**Step 3: Join the Project Tooling \- RL studio**

Once you join the project, please set up your account on RL Studio, which is where you will be completing all tasks. Please see the detailed RL Studio guide [here](). Please make sure you follow all instructions there carefully.

**Step 4: Review Project Ignite Instructions Manual**

Please spend no more than 1 hour reading through this Project Ignite Instructions Manual carefully. These instructions outline how tasks should be claimed, worked on and delivered for quality consistency.

### **Step 5: Begin Working**

Once you’ve completed all steps above:

* Start working on the tasks according to the Project Ignite Instruction Manual.  
* Log all time spent in **Insightful**.  
* Reach out to your Operations Lead on Slack if you have any questions or encounter blockers.

### **Support & Points of Contact**

**For IT-related issues:**

* **Okta/Slack Issues:** [support@mercor.com](mailto:support@mercor.com)   
* **Insightful Issues:** Please first review the guidelines linked above. If issues persist, contact [support@mercor.com](mailto:support@mercor.com) and [glowstonesupport@mercor.com](mailto:glowstonesupport@mercor.com). 

**For Project-related questions:**

If additional support is needed, please email the Project Leads:

* Brian Kim \- [briankim@mercor.com](mailto:briankim@mercor.com)   
* Samarthya Tomar \- [samarthyatomar@mercor.com](mailto:samarthyatomar@mercor.com)   
* Kailash Sundaram \- [kailash@mercor.com](mailto:kailash@mercor.com) 


We’re looking forward to seeing your contributions to **Project Ignite**. Welcome aboard\!

# ❓ FAQ

# **Frequently Asked Questions — Project Ignite (FAQ)**

**Q1: If/when we simulate pulling data from a web search using \[web link: \*paste entire URL\*\], are we supposed to put the placeholder with the URL in both the Notes and Golden Response, or just the Golden Response?**

A: Both.

**Q2: For tasks with open personas, is it okay to use one that isn't listed on previous tasks? Like QA Manager?**

A: Yes, allowed, as long as it matches your expertise.

**Q3: I'm looking to use file JIRA ForwardLane \- Data Sample Set \-12.5.25-anonymized.xlsx**  
**Qn \-\> Do we always use the first column to reference the record? Here, The first column is 'summary' containing strings e.g. 'Create New RIA signal' while the second one is Issue Key 'CRSP- 4086'**  
 **so, \[JIRA ForwardLane \- Data Sample Set \-12.5.25-anonymized.xlsx:"Create New RIA signal"\] or \[\[JIRA ForwardLane \- Data Sample Set \-12.5.25-anonymized.xlsx:CRSP-4086"\]**

A: I would use the unique identifier attached to the record. In this case, CRSP-4086 would be the better one

# 🤖 LLM Usage Policy

# **LLM Usage Policy**

Large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and similar tools **may not be used for task creation.** To preserve the integrity, authenticity, and accuracy of our work, the following uses are **prohibited**:

* **Rewriting or rephrasing your text:** LLM output can introduce non-human or overly polished language; your own voice is required.  
* **Writing rationales or explanations:** We expect your reasoning, not a model’s.  
* **Writing or reviewing prompts:** Prompt design must reflect your own skills and decision-making.  
* **Producing human responses:** All substantive responses must be authored by you.

# 💵 General Payment Information

# **Payments**

#### **General Payment Info**

* **Payouts** happen **weekly on Wednesdays** for work completed the previous week.  
* **No fixed working hours**, you get paid for whatever time you track (up to 40 hrs/week without approval).  
* **All legitimate work is paid** unless flagged for fraud, spam, or policy violations.  
* Refer to Mercor’s pay policy here: [https://talent.docs.mercor.com/policies/time-tracking-pay](https://talent.docs.mercor.com/policies/time-tracking-pay)

#### **Payment Providers**

1. Stripe: Our **primary payment platform** for ongoing contributors. Once you accept a job offer, you’ll be prompted to set up your **Stripe payout account** with basic personal and banking information. Visit the [Stripe payment](https://talent.docs.mercor.com/how-to/accept-stripe-payments) link for more details.  
      
2. [**Wise.com**](http://Wise.com)**:** Used mainly for **one-off payments** or for contributors not onboarded fully into Stripe. Simple to use, no Mercor account is needed to receive money via Wise. Visit the [Wise payment](https://talent.docs.mercor.com/how-to/accept-wise-payments) link for more details.

#### **When Will Payment be Received?**

##### Weekly Payout Schedule

**Payout Window:** Friday 00:00 PST through Friday 10:30 PST.

**Initiation Day:** Stripe batches and initiates all payouts on **Wednesday at 10:30 AM PST** for the prior week’s transactions.

##### Settlement Timing

**Returning Experts:**

- Funds typically **arrive in your bank account by Thursday** (i.e. within 1 business day of the Wednesday initiation)

**First-Time Payouts:**

- May take **3–5 business days** to complete due to identity and bank-account verification

**Subsequent Payouts:**

- After your first successful payout, transfers generally settle **even faster** (often within 1–2 business days)

**Notes & Edge Cases**

- If your bank observes its own holidays or cutoffs, settlement can be delayed beyond these targets  
- You’ll see the payout “in transit” on your Stripe dashboard immediately after Wednesday’s batch; “paid out” means it’s reached your bank

#### **FAQs**

**1\. What if my payment is delayed?**  
Check your email for any issues related to account verification or provider-side delays. Reach out to [glowstonesupport@mercor.com](mailto:glowstonesupport@mercor.com) if nothing is resolved within 3 business days.

**2\. I have received an incorrect amount.**  
First, confirm your hours were properly tracked with Insightful. If you still believe there’s an issue, reach out [glowstonesupport@mercor.com](mailto:glowstonesupport@mercor.com) with information like Project Name, hours worked, duration, SRT Queue you have worked on so that we can resolve your query as quickly as possible.

**3\. My bonus payment is missing.**  
Bonuses will not be reflected on [work.mercor.com](http://work.mercor.com) and will instead be directly paid out to your Stripe account. Once there, it will take 1-3 business days to be paid out to your bank account.
